lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 17 - ConvNets and TreeRNNs for NLP

unit.name: Kim CNN for sentence classification
unit.description: Covers the classic 2014 CNN architecture for sentence classification and the embedding fine-tuning issue it addressed.
unit.summary: The lecture presents Yoon Kim's 2014 sentence-classification CNN as the canonical early NLP use of convolutions, mainly for sentiment analysis and related classification tasks [ts=1101s][ts=1153s]. The architecture applies separate filters over bigrams, trigrams, and 4-grams, max-pools each filter's activations over the sentence, concatenates the pooled values, and feeds the resulting fixed-size vector into a simple softmax classifier [ts=1195s][ts=1232s][ts=1303s]. A major training issue is then discussed: when fine-tuning pretrained word vectors on a small supervised dataset, words observed in training move while unseen but semantically similar words stay in place, which can distort sentiment boundaries [ts=1262s][ts=1367s]. Kim's workaround is a dual-channel setup that keeps one embedding channel frozen and another fine-tuned, combining the benefits of task adaptation with preservation of the original semantic geometry [ts=1473s][ts=1500s]. The section closes by noting that the model was surprisingly strong for its simplicity, though some of its experimental advantage likely came from using dropout when many earlier baselines had not [ts=1612s][ts=1716s].

key_points.text: Kim's model uses convolutional filters of multiple widths over word vectors for sentence classification.
After sliding the filters across the sentence, max pooling keeps the strongest activation from each filter.
The concatenated pooled outputs are fed to a final softmax classifier for sentiment prediction.
Fine-tuning embeddings on a small labeled set can separate seen words from semantically similar unseen words and hurt generalization.
A two-channel embedding setup combines frozen pretrained vectors with fine-tuned vectors to get the best of both worlds.

kp_catalog.name: Kim 2014 sentence-classification CNN
kp_catalog.description: A multi-filter text CNN with max pooling and softmax classification that became the canonical early NLP CNN baseline.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Kim 2014 sentence-classification CNN captures the core idea that A multi-filter text CNN with max pooling and softmax classification that became the canonical early NLP CNN baseline.

kp_catalog.name: Frozen plus fine-tuned embedding channels
kp_catalog.description: Using both a static and an adaptive embedding channel mitigates harmful drift when fine-tuning on small labeled data.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Frozen plus fine-tuned embedding channels captures the core idea that Using both a static and an adaptive embedding channel mitigates harmful drift when fine-tuning on small labeled data.

kp_catalog.name: Pooling strategies in text CNNs
kp_catalog.description: Max, average, local, and k-max pooling determine how local activations are summarized across a sentence.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Pooling strategies in text CNNs captures the core idea that Max, average, local, and k-max pooling determine how local activations are summarized across a sentence.
